title: Location
summary: Where a business chooses to operate shapes its costs, its access to customers and suppliers, its brand and its risk — often for decades. This lesson works through the whole of syllabus 5.4: the quantitative factors that influence location and relocation (land and rent costs, labour costs, transport costs and proximity to the market and to suppliers, government incentives) and the qualitative factors (infrastructure, availability of skilled labour, ethical considerations and management preference); the reasons for and implications of outsourcing and subcontracting, offshoring, and reshoring; and the role of globalization and technology in modern location decisions. Every worked example is set out exactly the way our marking engine awards Business Management marks — AO1 knowledge, AO2 application to context and AO3 analysis and evaluation — so you can see why a supported judgement, not a memorised list of factors, is what earns the top band.
learningObjectives: Distinguish between quantitative and qualitative factors influencing the location and relocation of a business, and explain how each affects the decision.
Explain the reasons for and implications of outsourcing and subcontracting, offshoring, and reshoring.
Explain how globalization and technology have widened and changed the range of location options.
Analyse a location or relocation decision by applying the factors to a specific business and weighing them against each other.
Evaluate a location strategy — such as offshoring — by balancing costs against risks and reaching a supported judgement.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Location, location, location: picking the right spot
simpleExplanation.summary: Choosing where a business operates is a long-term, expensive commitment. The decision comes down to two kinds of factors: the ones you can put a number on (rent, wages, transport, grants) and the ones you cannot (infrastructure quality, skilled staff, ethics, what the owners want). The best location balances both against what that particular business needs to succeed.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of choosing a spot for a food van. Park on a quiet street and the pitch is free, but almost nobody walks past. Pay for a licence at a busy festival and the pitch costs far more, but hundreds of hungry customers stream by all day. The cheapest spot is rarely the best spot — a van selling to crowds needs footfall, while a van that supplies restaurants needs to be near its kitchens, not near shoppers. Which location wins depends entirely on how that van makes its money. Businesses face exactly the same trade-off, just with bigger numbers and longer leases.
steps: Work out what actually drives success for THIS business. A retailer lives or dies on footfall and proximity to customers; a factory cares more about labour costs, transport links and proximity to suppliers.
Gather the quantitative data — land and rent costs, wage rates, transport costs, any government grants or tax breaks — and compare the sites on the numbers.
Weigh the qualitative factors — infrastructure, the skills of the local workforce, ethical considerations and management preference — that the numbers do not capture.
Commit to a justified choice, saying which factor is decisive for this business and why, rather than simply picking the cheapest option.

flashcards.back: The measurable, usually financial factors that can be compared numerically between sites: land and rent costs, labour (wage) costs, transport costs, and quantifiable government incentives such as grants, subsidies and tax breaks.

The non-measurable factors that affect a location's success: quality of infrastructure, availability of skilled labour, ethical considerations, and management preference. Hard to put a number on, but often decisive for long-term success.

How close the business is to its customers. Vital for retailers and many services (footfall, fast delivery, customer convenience) and for bulk-gaining production where the finished product is costly to transport.

How close the business is to its raw materials or component suppliers. Critical for manufacturers, especially where inputs are heavy, bulky or perishable, because it cuts transport cost and lead time.
Financial or non-financial benefits a government offers to attract businesses to a region — grants, subsidies, tax breaks, cheap land or relaxed regulation — usually to create local jobs and growth.

The physical and organisational systems that support business activity: transport links (roads, ports, airports, rail), reliable utilities (power, water), and communications (broadband, mobile networks). A qualitative factor.
Paying an external, third-party organisation to perform a task or function the business could do in-house. It is about WHO does the work; the provider can be domestic or overseas.
Relocating a business process or operation to ANOTHER COUNTRY, usually to cut costs or reach a new market. It is about WHERE the work is done; the business may still own the operation itself.
Bringing back to the home country an operation that was previously offshored. Often driven by rising overseas wages, quality problems, long lead times, supply-chain risk or reputational concerns.

The growing integration of world markets, capital and supply chains. It widens the set of viable locations, making offshoring and international outsourcing possible and intensifying competition between locations.

Digital communications, automation and e-commerce weaken the tie between a business and a single physical site, enabling remote work and 'footloose' firms, while automation can reduce the labour-cost advantage that drove offshoring.

Offshoring = another COUNTRY (where). Outsourcing = another COMPANY (who). You can offshore without outsourcing (own factory abroad) and outsource without offshoring (a domestic contractor). They are not synonyms.

Recommending the lowest-cost site while ignoring qualitative factors is a classic error. A cheap location with poor infrastructure, an unskilled workforce or ethical risks can cost far more in the long run than it saves.

quickCheck.prompt: Which of the following is a QUANTITATIVE location factor rather than a qualitative one?
options: The quality of local road and broadband infrastructure
The availability of skilled labour in the area
The annual rent and business rates for the site
The management team's personal preference for the city
quickCheck.answer: The annual rent and business rates for the site

quickCheck.prompt: A UK clothing brand closes its own British factory and pays an independent factory in Vietnam to make its garments. This decision is BEST described as:
options: Reshoring, because production has changed location
Both offshoring and outsourcing, because production moved to another country AND to another company
Outsourcing only, because a third party now does the work in any location
Offshoring only, because the brand still owns the Vietnamese factory
quickCheck.answer: Both offshoring and outsourcing, because production moved to another country AND to another company

quickCheck.prompt: A firm that moved its call centre abroad five years ago now closes it and reopens the call centre in its home country, citing customer complaints about service quality. This is an example of:
options: Offshoring
Reshoring
Outsourcing
Subcontracting

quickCheck.prompt: For a NEW HIGH-STREET COFFEE SHOP, which location factor is likely to matter MOST?
options: Proximity to coffee-bean growers to cut input transport costs
Pedestrian footfall and proximity to the target market
Access to a deep-water port for exporting
The lowest possible warehouse rent on an out-of-town estate
quickCheck.answer: Pedestrian footfall and proximity to the target market

quickCheck.prompt: A cement manufacturer uses heavy, bulky raw materials that are expensive to transport. Which factor should weigh MOST heavily in its location decision?
options: Proximity to its final retail customers
Prestige and brand image of the location
Proximity to its suppliers of raw materials
Being in a fashionable 'creative hub' district
quickCheck.answer: Proximity to its suppliers of raw materials

faq.q: What is the difference between outsourcing and offshoring?
faq.a: They are related but not the same. Offshoring is about WHERE the work is done — in another country. Outsourcing is about WHO does the work — a separate, third-party company. You can outsource to a firm in your own country (domestic outsourcing) and you can offshore without outsourcing by opening your own facility abroad. When a business pays a foreign contractor, it is doing both at once. Keeping the 'where' and the 'who' separate in your mind is the fastest way to avoid the most common 5.4 mistake.

faq.q: What is the difference between offshoring and reshoring?
faq.a: Offshoring moves an operation OUT of the home country to another country, usually to save costs. Reshoring (also called onshoring) is the reverse — bringing that operation BACK to the home country. Businesses reshore when the original savings shrink or backfire: rising overseas wages, poor quality control, long and fragile supply chains, high shipping costs, or reputational damage from ethical concerns can all tip the balance back towards home.

faq.q: Is a quantitative or a qualitative analysis more important?
faq.a: Neither on its own is enough — a strong decision needs both. A purely quantitative decision might land the business in the cheapest site, only for poor infrastructure or an unskilled workforce to cripple it. A purely qualitative decision might pick a wonderful location the business cannot actually afford. The marks — and good decisions — come from integrating both and saying which factor is decisive for the specific business in front of you.

faq.q: How have globalization and technology changed location decisions?
faq.a: Globalization has widened the map: falling trade barriers, container shipping and integrated supply chains make offshoring and international outsourcing realistic for even medium-sized firms, and locations now compete with each other for investment. Technology loosens the tie to any single site — video calls, cloud tools and e-commerce let 'footloose' firms and remote teams operate almost anywhere, while automation can shrink the labour-cost gap that made offshoring attractive, which is one reason some firms reshore.

faq.q: How do I use location concepts in a Paper 1 or Paper 2 exam?
faq.a: You will be given a case study, so anchor everything to it. If the stimulus mentions high staff turnover, link it to the availability of skilled labour; if it gives cost figures, use them; if it mentions overseas quality problems, that is a reshoring cue. Always say what a factor means for THIS business, weigh the factors against each other, and — for 'evaluate' or 'recommend' commands — finish with a supported judgement rather than a list.

sections.content: The decision of where to locate — or whether to relocate — is one of the most consequential a business makes. It is a long-term, capital-heavy commitment that shapes costs, revenues, brand image, supply-chain risk and even the ethics for which the firm becomes known. Syllabus 5.4 asks you to weigh two families of factors: the QUANTITATIVE ones you can put a number on (land and rent, labour costs, transport, government incentives) and the QUALITATIVE ones you cannot (infrastructure, skilled labour, ethics, management preference). It then extends the idea across borders — outsourcing and subcontracting, offshoring and reshoring — and asks how globalization and technology have reshaped the whole map. Because 5.4 is an evaluation-heavy topic, the worked examples below are set out the way our marking engine actually awards marks, so you can see why a supported judgement outscores a memorised list of factors every time.

Quantitative factors are the measurable, usually financial aspects of a location decision. Because they can be expressed as numbers, they can be compared directly between sites and built into a cost calculation. They are essential — but they only tell part of the story, and choosing purely on cost is one of the errors examiners penalise most.

items: **Land and rent costs:** the price of buying or leasing the site, plus business rates. City-centre sites cost far more than out-of-town or rural ones — a gap that can decide the location for a cost-sensitive operation.
**Labour costs:** the local wage rates for the skill level the business needs. A major driver of offshoring, though low wages mean little if productivity or skills are also low.

**Transport costs:** the cost of moving raw materials IN and finished goods OUT. This is where proximity to suppliers and proximity to the market become quantitative — nearness cuts freight cost and lead time.

**Proximity to the market and to suppliers:** being near customers cuts distribution cost and supports fast service (crucial for retailers); being near suppliers cuts input transport cost and lead time (crucial for manufacturers using heavy or perishable inputs).

**Government incentives:** quantifiable grants, subsidies, tax breaks or cheap land offered by governments to attract businesses and jobs to a region — a direct, measurable reduction in the cost of a site.

sections.question:
A bakery, 'Crumbly & Co.', is choosing between two locations for a new production facility and expects to produce 50,000 units per year.

**Location A (urban):** Fixed costs (rent, rates) £100,000 per year; variable costs (labour, ingredients) £3.00 per unit.
**Location B (rural):** Fixed costs (rent, rates) £60,000 per year; variable costs (labour, ingredients) £3.50 per unit.

Calculate the total annual cost for each location and recommend which Crumbly & Co. should choose based solely on these costs. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**

**Step 1 — Total cost for Location A.**
Total cost = fixed costs + (variable cost per unit × units)
= £100,000 + (£3.00 × 50,000)
= £100,000 + £150,000 = **£250,000**

**Step 2 — Total cost for Location B.**
= £60,000 + (£3.50 × 50,000)
= £60,000 + £175,000 = **£235,000**

**Step 3 — Compare and recommend.**
Location B (£235,000) is £15,000 cheaper per year than Location A (£250,000) at the expected output of 50,000 units. Based purely on these quantitative costs, Crumbly & Co. should choose **Location B**.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** — this is a 'calculate/recommend on the numbers' task, so the engine credits correct method and a supported numerical conclusion:

- **AO2 (1):** correct method for Location A (fixed + variable × units) with working shown.
- **AO2 (1):** correct total for Location A = £250,000.
- **AO2 (1):** correct total for Location B = £235,000.
- **AO4 (1):** a recommendation that USES the figures — Location B, because it is £15,000 cheaper at 50,000 units.

The engine rewards clear working and correct units, not just the final number: a bare '£235,000' with no method or no recommendation forfeits marks, and note the '**based solely on these costs**' framing flags that a fuller answer would still need the qualitative factors below.

sections.content: When a question asks you to calculate, show every step: state the formula, substitute the numbers, and give the answer with correct units (£, $, €). Marks are awarded for the process, not just the final figure — and if a site is close but qualitative factors differ, say so, because examiners reward candidates who notice that the cheapest number is not automatically the right decision.

Qualitative factors are the non-numerical, 'softer' aspects of a location — the quality of the business environment rather than its price. They are hard to measure but often decisive for long-term success and sustainability, and ignoring them to chase short-term cost savings is a classic business (and exam) error.

items: **Infrastructure:** the quality of transport links (roads, ports, airports, rail), reliable utilities (power, water) and communications (broadband, mobile). Weak infrastructure can wipe out a wage saving through delays and downtime.

**Availability of skilled labour:** not just the cost of labour but whether the local workforce has the skills, education and reliability the business needs. A pool of skilled staff (as in an industry cluster) can be worth paying higher wages for.

**Ethical considerations:** local labour laws and working conditions, environmental regulation, and the reputational risk of being associated with poor practice. A location that saves money but exposes the brand to ethical criticism can cost far more in lost trust.

**Management preference:** the owners' or directors' own views — a wish to stay near home, family or an existing community, or industrial inertia that keeps a firm where it has always been even when the original reasons have faded.

**Proximity to market or suppliers (qualitative dimension):** beyond transport cost, being in the right place can enhance brand image (a luxury watchmaker in Switzerland) or embed a firm in a cluster of specialist suppliers and customers.

sections.question:
A tech start-up is choosing between two cities. It has identified three key qualitative factors and given each a weight (1–5, where 5 is most important), then scored each city on each factor (1–10, where 10 is best).

Access to skilled graduates (weight 5): City X = 9, City Y = 6
Local transport infrastructure (weight 3): City X = 7, City Y = 8
'Creative hub' image (weight 4): City X = 8, City Y = 5

Using a weighted score analysis, recommend a city for the start-up. [6]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
A weighted score multiplies each factor's score by its weight and sums the results, so the factors the business cares about most count for more.

**City X:** (9 × 5) + (7 × 3) + (8 × 4) = 45 + 21 + 32 = **98**
**City Y:** (6 × 5) + (8 × 3) + (5 × 4) = 30 + 24 + 20 = **74**

City X scores 98 against City Y's 74, so the start-up should choose **City X**. The reasoning matters as much as the total: City Y actually has the better transport infrastructure, but that is the least heavily weighted factor. City X wins decisively on the two factors the start-up rates most highly — access to skilled graduates and a creative-hub image — which are exactly the qualitative strengths a young technology firm depends on to recruit talent and build a reputation. The weighting has therefore steered the decision towards strategic fit rather than a single strong feature.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 6 marks:**

- **AO2 (up to 2):** correct method and calculation of both weighted totals (98 and 74) with working shown.
- **AO2 (1):** application of the technique to THIS business — recognising that graduates and image are what a tech start-up most needs.
- **AO3 (up to 2):** analysis of WHY City X wins despite City Y's better infrastructure — the trade-off created by the weights, not just the raw scores.
- **AO4 (1):** a clear, supported recommendation of City X justified by the weighted result and the start-up's priorities.

The engine rewards interpretation, not just arithmetic: a candidate who computes 98 vs 74 but never explains why the weighting favours City X, or who ignores that City Y beat it on infrastructure, is held below the top band even with the numbers correct.

sections.content: Once a business thinks beyond a single site, three related strategies come into play — and the syllabus expects you to separate them precisely. OUTSOURCING (also called subcontracting) is about WHO does the work: paying an external, third-party firm to perform a function rather than doing it in-house. OFFSHORING is about WHERE the work is done: relocating an operation to another country. These two axes are independent — you can outsource to a domestic contractor (outsourcing without offshoring), or open your own factory abroad (offshoring without outsourcing), or pay a foreign contractor (both at once). RESHORING is the reversal of offshoring: bringing an operation back to the home country. Confusing the 'where' with the 'who' is the single most penalised error in 5.4.

items: **Outsourcing / subcontracting — reasons:** access specialist expertise, convert fixed costs into variable costs, free managers to focus on core activities, and flex capacity up or down. **Implications:** less direct control over quality and timing, dependence on the provider, and possible loss of in-house skills.

**Offshoring — reasons:** lower labour and land costs, access to raw materials or new markets, and sometimes lighter regulation. **Implications:** longer and more fragile supply chains, communication and cultural barriers, quality-control risk, job losses at home, and reputational/ethical exposure.

**Reshoring — reasons:** rising overseas wages that erode the saving, quality problems, long lead times, supply-chain disruption, high shipping costs, and the PR and quality benefits of a 'made at home' story. **Implications:** higher labour costs but greater control, shorter lead times and reduced reputational risk.
**The key distinctions:** offshoring = country (where); outsourcing = company (who); reshoring = bringing it back home. Keep these three straight and most 5.4 questions become manageable.

sections.content: The role of globalization and technology

Modern location decisions are inseparable from globalization and technology. GLOBALIZATION — falling trade barriers, container shipping, integrated supply chains and freer flows of capital — has widened the map, making offshoring and international outsourcing realistic even for medium-sized firms and forcing locations to compete for investment with incentives. TECHNOLOGY pulls in two directions. On one hand, digital communications, cloud tools and e-commerce weaken the tie between a business and any single physical site: 'footloose' firms and remote teams can operate almost anywhere, and an online retailer may need only a well-placed warehouse rather than a high-street presence. On the other hand, automation can shrink the labour-cost advantage that first drove work overseas — if robots do the assembly, low foreign wages matter less — which is one reason a wave of firms has reshored production closer to their customers.

For higher-level analysis (AO3), never just list the pros and cons of a location or of offshoring. Explain WHY a factor is an advantage or a disadvantage FOR THIS SPECIFIC BUSINESS in its context — a wage saving means one thing to a low-margin manufacturer and another to a premium brand whose customers care about ethics. Then conclude with a justified recommendation that acknowledges the trade-offs, because the evaluation marks live in the judgement, not the list.

items: **Confusing offshoring with outsourcing** — offshoring is about the COUNTRY (where the work happens); outsourcing is about the COMPANY (who does it). Treating them as synonyms, or assuming offshoring always means using a foreign contractor, loses marks.

**Mixing up quantitative and qualitative factors** — rent, wages, transport costs and grants are QUANTITATIVE (measurable); infrastructure quality, skilled-labour availability, ethics and management preference are QUALITATIVE. Putting a factor in the wrong column signals shaky understanding.

**Recommending the cheapest location automatically** — the lowest-cost site is not always best. A robust answer balances the numbers against qualitative factors and explains why a dearer location may deliver better long-term value.

**Ignoring what the business actually does** — a retailer needs proximity to the MARKET and footfall; a heavy-goods manufacturer needs proximity to SUPPLIERS and transport links. Applying a factory's priorities to a shop (or vice versa) shows no application.
**Treating offshoring as risk-free cost-cutting** — it brings quality-control, lead-time, supply-chain and ethical/reputational risks that a top-band evaluation must weigh, not just the wage saving.
**Listing factors without applying them** — a bare list of location factors earns AO1 only; the marks climb when each factor is tied to the specific business in the case.
**Evaluating without a supported judgement** — an 'evaluate' or 'recommend' answer that gives both sides but never commits to a justified conclusion cannot reach the top band.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

Business Management 5.4 is assessed against the objectives AO1 (knowledge and understanding), AO2 (application to the specific business), AO3 (analysis and evaluation) and, in extended answers, AO4 (structure and a supported judgement). In the analytic/points scheme each distinct valid point earns credit, but the higher 'evaluate' and 'recommend' marks are reserved for answers that combine APPLICATION to context with a BALANCED evaluation that ends in a SUPPORTED JUDGEMENT. Watch how the marks below attach to applied, two-sided reasoning and a justified conclusion — never to a generic list of offshoring pros and cons.

sections.question: Evaluate whether a manufacturing business should offshore its production to a lower-cost country. [10]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.** (Take a mid-market manufacturer — say a home-appliances maker facing intense price competition — as the context.)

The strongest argument for offshoring is cost. Labour and land are typically far cheaper in a lower-cost country, so relocating production could sharply reduce the manufacturer's unit costs. For a business under price pressure in a competitive appliance market, that saving could restore its margins or let it cut prices to win market share, and it may also bring the firm closer to fast-growing overseas markets and cheaper raw materials. If cost is the main threat to the business's survival, offshoring directly attacks it.

Offshoring can also add flexibility and scale. Producing in an established manufacturing hub abroad may give access to specialist suppliers and a large, experienced workforce, allowing the firm to expand output faster than it could at home.

However, the savings come with serious risks. Quality control is harder to maintain at a distance, and for an appliance maker whose reputation depends on reliability, a rise in faults could damage the brand far more than the wage saving is worth. Lead times lengthen: goods shipped from another continent take weeks to arrive, making the firm slower to respond to demand and more exposed to supply-chain disruption such as shipping delays or geopolitical shocks. There are ethical and reputational risks too — if the lower-cost country has weaker labour or environmental standards, customers and campaigners may accuse the firm of exploiting workers, harming the very brand it relies on. And offshoring means cutting jobs at home, which can trigger negative publicity and damage staff morale.

Much depends on the type of product and customer. If the appliances are simple, price-sensitive and easy to make to a consistent standard, the quality risk is low and the cost case is compelling. If they are premium products sold partly on a 'made at home' or ethical reputation, offshoring could destroy the very value proposition that justifies their price. The firm should also consider whether automation at home could deliver similar cost savings WITHOUT the supply-chain and reputational risks — increasingly a reason manufacturers reshore.

**Judgement.** On balance, this manufacturer should offshore ONLY if two conditions hold: the cost pressure genuinely threatens its competitiveness, and its products can be made abroad to a reliable standard without undermining a quality- or ethics-based brand. For a mass-market, price-driven appliance maker fighting on cost, the labour saving is likely to be decisive and offshoring is justified, provided it invests in tight quality control and vets suppliers' labour standards to protect its reputation. For a premium or ethically positioned manufacturer, the reputational and quality risks are more likely to outweigh the saving, and reshoring-style investment in home automation would be the wiser route. The right answer therefore hinges on the firm's competitive position and brand — not on the wage gap alone.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 10 marks:**

- **AO1 — knowledge:** the engine credits accurate command of the core concepts the question turns on — offshoring as relocating production to another country, the cost drivers (labour, land), and the associated risks (quality control, lead time, supply-chain, ethical/reputational, home job losses), plus awareness of reshoring and automation as alternatives. Correct terminology signals AO1.
- **AO2 — application to THIS business:** marks are awarded for tying those concepts to the specific manufacturer — its price-competitive market, its reliance on reliability/reputation, the product type — rather than discussing offshoring in the abstract. Generic points that never mention this firm do not earn AO2.
- **AO3 — analysis and balanced evaluation:** the engine credits developed two-sided reasoning — cost savings, market access and scale WEIGHED AGAINST quality, lead-time, supply-chain and ethical/reputational risks — and the exploration of what the decision depends on (product type, brand positioning, automation alternative).
- **AO4 — supported judgement:** the top marks require a conclusion that COMMITS to a position and justifies WHY one side outweighs the other for this context — here, that the decision hinges on the firm's competitive position and brand, with clear conditions attached. A 'judgement' that merely restates both sides without choosing cannot reach the top band.
- **What the engine rejects:** all-benefits or all-drawbacks answers with no balance; a long generic list of offshoring pros and cons with no link to the business; and correct definitions that never build towards a decision.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — AO1 for the right location concepts (offshoring, quantitative vs qualitative factors, reshoring), AO2 for applying them to the business in the question, and AO3 for balanced analysis ending in a supported judgement. So do not just list the advantages and disadvantages of offshoring: name the concept, apply it to the specific manufacturer, weigh cost savings against quality, lead-time, ethical and reputational risks, and finish by committing to a recommendation with a reason. The application and the judgement are where the top-band marks are won.

Location links directly to the rest of operations management and beyond. The cost calculations here feed into production methods, capacity and cost/revenue analysis; the offshoring and reshoring debate connects to supply-chain management, quality control and sustainability; and the ethical and reputational threads run into stakeholder analysis and CSR. Master the habit built in this lesson — separate quantitative from qualitative factors, keep offshoring, outsourcing and reshoring distinct, apply each to the specific business, weigh both sides, then commit to a justified judgement — and you have the template that earns marks across every evaluation question in Business Management.

sections.label: Get a Paper 2 question marked: evaluate a location or offshoring decision, applying the factors to the business and reaching a supported judgement

items.label: The Economist explains: What is reshoring?
